methodology: Global frontiers: parsed tasks/FRONTIER.md (active list) and tasks/FRONTIER-ARCHIVE.md for RESOLVED/MERGED/PARTIALLY_RESOLVED events in S400-S420. Domain frontiers: parsed all 43 domains/*/tasks/FRONTIER.md resolved tables for S400-S420 entries. Linkage: checked domain frontier text for references to global frontier IDs (F-NK1, F-META1 etc). Prior artifacts: f-nk6-governance-baseline-s417.json and f-nk6-namespace-linkage-s418.json used for reconciliation.

global_resolution_events.note: Merged into F-SUB1, publication path absorbed

global_resolution_note: Includes RESOLVED + MERGED + PARTIALLY_RESOLVED. If strict (RESOLVED only): 0.048/session. Prior S417 artifact measured 0.095/session for S397-S417 window using similar inclusive methodology. L-912 baseline was 0.08/session at N=572.
resolution_ratio_note: Domain/global = 0.81 / 0.143 = 5.7x. Prior S417 measurement found 17.5x ratio using stricter global-only counting (domain frontiers only vs strictly global). Both measurements confirm large gap.

domain_first_protocol.description: Domain-first dispatch routes DOMEX work primarily to specific domain frontiers (currently 79% domain-first without any explicit rule). The binding gap is not routing frequency but the absence of upward namespace linkage: 97% of domain frontiers never reference global frontiers, so domain resolutions do not trigger global synthesis. The protocol must add a bridging mechanism: each domain frontier should name its parent global question, and each global frontier should decompose into >=1 domain sub-questions.
domain_first_protocol.dispatch_rule: When opening a domain FRONTIER.md entry, name the parent global frontier from tasks/FRONTIER.md it feeds into (or explicitly mark as domain-only with no global parent). When closing a domain frontier as RESOLVED/CONFIRMED/FALSIFIED, check whether the parent global frontier is now resolvable given the new evidence.
domain_first_protocol.global_synthesis_cadence: Every 5 sessions, review global frontiers that have 2+ linked domain frontier resolutions since last review; attempt synthesis update or partial resolution. This is the 'REM sleep' analogue: consolidation of accumulated domain knowledge into global understanding.
domain_first_protocol.expected_improvement: Raising linkage rate from 3% to 25% should improve global frontier resolution rate from 0.14/session toward 0.20/session (+40%) based on the federated convergence model (L-926, P-274). Full 0.24/session target requires both linkage and domain work being routed to globally-relevant questions, not just domain-local ones.

prior_artifacts: f-nk6-governance-baseline-s417.json (S397-S417 window, found 0.095 global/session, 1.67 domain/session, 17.5x ratio, 4.1% linkage)
f-nk6-namespace-linkage-s418.json (S418 hardening, confirmed 3.0% domain->global linkage, 27.8% global->domain)
f-nk6-governance-model-s415.json (S415 governance analysis, found 2.16x domain improvement post-S400, 0 global improvement)
verdict_detail: The F-NK6 hypothesis has two components: (1) domain-first dispatch is already the default pattern (79% DOMEX dispatches are domain-focused, CONFIRMED pre-S400). (2) Formalizing domain-first protocol will improve global frontier resolution >=50% (from 0.16/session to >=0.24/session). Component 2 is NOT YET CONFIRMED. Global resolution rate S400-S420 = 0.143/session (above L-912 0.08 baseline but below 0.24 target). The root cause is namespace disconnection (3% linkage), not dispatch routing. PENDING full test: implement federated convergence model (explicit parent-global fields in domain frontiers) and measure over next 20 sessions.
notes: Three key findings: (1) Domain frontier resolution is consistently 5-18x faster than global (0.81 vs 0.143/session), confirming the structural asymmetry that motivates F-NK6. (2) The bottleneck is namespace isolation (3% linkage), not dispatch frequency — swarm is already 79-97% domain-first. (3) Global resolutions that did occur in S400-S420 were TTL cleanups (F105, F-PUB1, F-PERS1) not organic research closures, suggesting global frontiers are being administratively managed rather than genuinely resolved. The domain-first protocol must add upward namespace bridges, not just change dispatch frequency. Cites: L-912, L-918, L-926, P-274.
